exam.title: Valid Subarrays With Exactly One Peak - Demo Test
exam.description: Comprehensive test covering array manipulation, peak finding algorithms, and subarray analysis techniques

questions.title: Peak Element Definition
questions.prompt: What is the definition of a peak element in an array?
options: An element that is greater than its immediate neighbors
An element that is greater than or equal to its immediate neighbors
The maximum element in the entire array
An element that is greater than all elements to its right
questions.explanation: A peak element is one that is greater than its immediate neighbors. For edge elements, only one neighbor needs to be considered.

questions.title: Valid Peak Characteristics
questions.prompt: Which of the following are valid characteristics of a peak element? (Select all that apply)
options: Can be at the beginning of the array
Can be at the end of the array
Must be greater than both neighbors
questions.explanation: A peak can be at array boundaries, can have equal neighbors, and must be greater than (or equal to) its neighbors. It doesn't have to be the global maximum.

questions.title: Single Element Peak
questions.prompt: True or False: A single-element array always has exactly one peak.
questions.explanation: True. A single element is considered a peak since it has no neighbors to compare with.

questions.title: Peak Finding Complexity
questions.prompt: The time complexity of finding a peak element using binary search is O(___.
questions.explanation: Binary search can find a peak element in O(log n) time by comparing middle elements and deciding which half must contain a peak.

questions.title: Valid Subarray Count
questions.prompt: In the array [1, 3, 2, 4, 1], how many subarrays have exactly one peak?
questions.explanation: The subarrays with exactly one peak are: [1,3,2], [1,3,2,4], [3,2], [3,2,4], [3,2,4,1], [2,4], [2,4,1]. Total: 7 subarrays.

questions.title: Algorithm Steps
questions.prompt: Arrange the steps for finding a peak using binary search in correct order:
options: If middle is peak, return it
If left neighbor is greater, search left half
If right neighbor is greater, search right half
questions.explanation: The correct order is: Choose middle → Compare with neighbors → If peak, return → If left greater, search left → If right greater, search right.

questions.title: Monotonic Array Peaks
questions.prompt: In a strictly increasing array [1,2,3,4,5], how many subarrays have exactly one peak?
questions.explanation: In a strictly increasing array, each subarray's last element is a peak. There are 5 single-element subarrays + 4 two-element subarrays = 9 total.

questions.title: Equal Neighbors
questions.prompt: True or False: In array [2,2,2], all elements are peaks.
questions.explanation: True. When elements are equal, each can be considered a peak since none is strictly greater than its neighbors.

questions.title: Multiple Peaks Count
questions.prompt: In array [1,5,2,6,3,7,4], how many peak elements exist?
questions.explanation: The peak elements are: 5 (greater than 1 and 2), 6 (greater than 2 and 3), and 7 (greater than 3 and 4). Total: 3 peaks.

questions.title: Optimal Approaches
questions.prompt: Which approaches are suitable for counting subarrays with exactly one peak? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Greedy approach (expanding from peaks) and brute force are suitable. DP and divide-and-conquer are overly complex for this problem.

questions.title: Equal Neighbors
questions.prompt: If an array has equal adjacent elements [2,2,2], how many peaks does it have?
questions.explanation: When elements are equal, each can be considered a peak since none is strictly greater than its neighbors. All three elements are peaks.

questions.title: Optimal Strategy
questions.prompt: Which strategy is most efficient for finding all valid subarrays?
questions.explanation: Expanding from each peak element is most efficient as it directly targets valid subarrays rather than checking all possibilities.

questions.title: Valley Elements
questions.prompt: What is a valley element in an array?
options: An element smaller than its neighbors
The minimum element in the array
An element at the beginning of the array
questions.explanation: A valley element is one that is smaller than its immediate neighbors, the opposite of a peak element.

questions.title: Google Interview Context
questions.prompt: Why is this problem popular in Google interviews?
questions.explanation: This problem is popular because it tests array manipulation, has multiple solution approaches (brute force, greedy, optimized), and combines multiple algorithmic concepts.

questions.title: Subarray Length Constraint
questions.prompt: What is the minimum length of a subarray that can have exactly one peak?
questions.explanation: A single-element subarray has exactly one peak (the element itself), so the minimum length is 1.

questions.title: Microsoft Interview Focus
questions.prompt: What aspect of this problem is Microsoft most interested in?
questions.explanation: Microsoft typically focuses on time complexity analysis and optimization in array problems like this.

questions.title: Bloomberg Application
questions.prompt: How might this concept be applied in financial data analysis at Bloomberg?
options: Finding local maxima in stock prices
questions.explanation: Finding local maxima (peaks) in stock price data is a direct application of this concept in financial analysis.

questions.title: Array Size Impact
questions.prompt: How does the time complexity change as array size increases?
questions.explanation: With an optimal O(n) solution, the time complexity increases linearly with array size.

questions.title: Memory Usage
questions.prompt: For an array of size n=1000, what's the approximate memory usage of an optimal solution?
questions.explanation: With O(1) space complexity, the memory usage is minimal, approximately 1KB for counters and variables.

questions.title: Debugging Scenario
questions.prompt: If your algorithm returns 0 for array [1,2,1], what's the most likely bug?
questions.explanation: The array [1,2,1] has valid subarrays with peaks, so returning 0 suggests the subarray counting logic is incorrect.

questions.title: Performance Comparison
questions.prompt: Compared to brute force, how much faster is the optimal solution for n=10000?
questions.explanation: Brute force is O(n³) while optimal is O(n), giving approximately 100x speedup for n=10000.

questions.title: Real-world Analogy
questions.prompt: Which real-world scenario is analogous to finding peaks in subarrays?
options: Finding hills in a mountain range
questions.explanation: All these scenarios involve finding local maxima in sequential data, making them analogous to the peak finding problem.

questions.title: Algorithm Selection
questions.prompt: When would you prefer a simpler but slower algorithm?
questions.explanation: For small input sizes, a simpler algorithm might be preferred despite being slower, due to easier implementation and maintenance.

questions.title: Testing Strategy
questions.prompt: What's the most important test case for this problem?
questions.explanation: All these edge cases are crucial for testing the robustness of the solution.

questions.title: Future Enhancement
questions.prompt: How could this problem be extended for more complexity?
questions.explanation: All these extensions would add interesting complexity and new algorithmic challenges to the base problem.
